request_debug.prompt_preview:
You rerank dead-startup candidates for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and K candidates retrieved by hybrid search, score each candidate from four perspectives, then return ONLY the top 5 ranked from most to least relevant.

Perspectives (each scored 0-10 with a one-sentence rationale):
- business_model: how similar are the revenue, pricing, and customer-relationship shapes?
- market: how similar are the target buyers, market size, and demand drivers?
- gtm: how similar are the go-to-

response.text: {"ranked":[{"candidate_id":"38-studios::gaming","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"38 Studios built a premium single-player RPG and an unfinished MMO—no free-to-play, no cosmetic monetization, no battle pass—so the revenue shape is quite different despite being gaming."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both target consumers who play games globally, though 38 Studios focused on core console/PC RPG/MMO players rather than the mobile-plus-PC 4X audience."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"38 Studios relied on a major publisher (EA Partners) for retail/digital distribution, whereas the pitch uses a self-published F2P model across app stores and Steam."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"38 Studios was a multi-hundred-person studio with a shipped AAA title, closer in scale to an early growth stage, though it also burned through enormous capital before failure."}},"rationale":"38 Studios is the closest gaming peer—it failed while developing a large-scale consumer game—but its premium/publisher-driven model and AAA RPG/MMO focus diverge meaningfully from a F2P 4X mobile/Steam title monetized by cosmetics and a battle pass."},{"candidate_id":"onlive::gaming","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"OnLive was a cloud-streaming service that pivoted to free-to-play with à la carte purchases and a subscription bundle—distribution and revenue mechanics differ substantially from a game studio selling cosmetics and a battle pass."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"OnLive targeted PC/console gamers seeking convenience, overlapping only loosely with the 4X strategy player base that spans mobile and desktop."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"OnLive sold access to a streaming platform requiring hardware partnerships and ISP-quality awareness, a very different channel motion from publishing a F2P title on iOS, Android, and Steam."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"OnLive raised far more capital and operated infrastructure at a scale incomparable to a mid-stage F2P game studio, though both faced consumer adoption challenges before failure."}},"rationale":"OnLive's gaming adjacency is relevant, but it was a cloud-streaming infrastructure play rather than a game-development studio, making business model, GTM, and scale comparisons weak."},{"candidate_id":"quibi::media_content","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Quibi used a subscription paywall for short-form video content—a fundamentally different model from F2P cosmetics/battle-pass monetization despite both being consumer digital entertainment."},"market":{"score":4,"rationale":"Quibi targeted mobile-first media consumers, which overlaps tangentially with mobile gamers, but the demand drivers (episodic content vs. interactive strategy games) differ significantly."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both used app-store distribution on iOS and Android targeting a broad consumer base, but Quibi's GTM was dominated by massive marketing spend and content partnerships rather than organic game discovery."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Quibi launched with heavy funding and shut down within six months, a rapid high-burn failure somewhat analogous to an over-funded F2P title that fails to retain players."}},"rationale":"Quibi shares the mobile consumer entertainment space and app-store distribution, but its subscription content model and non-interactive product make it a weak analogue to a F2P 4X strategy game."},{"candidate_id":"friendster::social_communication","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Friendster was an ad-supported social network that later pivoted to social gaming—no direct parallel to a standalone F2P game with cosmetic/battle-pass monetization."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Friendster's pivot to social gaming in 2011 shows some overlap with casual/social game audiences, but its core market was social networking users, not strategy gamers."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Friendster grew through social network virality and web-based engagement, entirely unlike distributing a native game across iOS, Android, and Steam."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Friendster was a mature, large-scale social platform when it failed—not comparable to an early-stage game studio shipping its first title."}},"rationale":"Friendster's late pivot to social gaming gives it a thin connection, but the business model, market, GTM, and scale are all very different from the pitch."},{"candidate_id":"jawbone::hardware","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":1,"rationale":"Jawbone sold physical consumer hardware (headsets, speakers, fitness trackers) at premium price points—nothing resembling F2P game monetization."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Jawbone targeted mainstream consumers interested in personal electronics, with negligible overlap with 4X strategy game players."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Jawbone used retail channels and online direct sales for hardware, a completely different distribution motion from app stores and Steam for a digital game."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Jawbone was a late-stage, heavily funded consumer hardware company when it collapsed, not analogous to an early-stage game studio."}},"rationale":"Jawbone is included only for completeness as a B2C consumer product failure; it shares almost no meaningful similarity with a F2P 4X strategy gaming studio across any of the four perspectives."}]}
